topic: temple visit — first since Eunji passed, very emotional

turns.content: I went to the temple today. Beomeosa, in the mountains above the city. It was the first time I have been since my wife passed away. I did not plan it — I simply woke this morning and felt that it was time. I am not sure what I expected, but I think I want to talk about it.

I'm glad you felt ready, and I'm glad you went. Sometimes the body or some deeper instinct decides before the conscious mind catches up.

Tell me about it — whatever feels important. The visit itself, what you felt, what surprised you.

The path up to the temple is lined with old pine trees, and they have not changed. That was the first thing I noticed — the indifference of the trees. Years pass, a person disappears from the world, and the pines are exactly where they were. I think I found that comforting rather than cruel. The constancy of it. Eunji and I used to walk that path on Sundays. She would always stop at the same bend to catch her breath, and I would wait without saying anything, because she did not like to be reminded that the hill was steep.

That image of waiting silently at the bend is very tender — the kind of small, practiced kindness that only develops over decades. You knew her well enough to know that pointing out the hill would have bothered her more than the hill itself.

And you're right about the trees. There's something grounding about a landscape that doesn't register human absence. It can feel dismissive, but it can also feel like permission — the world continued, and so can you.

What was it like when you reached the temple itself?

The smell was the same. Incense and old wood. I think smell is the most disloyal of the senses — it bypasses reason entirely and delivers you into the past without permission. I stepped into the main hall and the scent hit me, and for a moment I was standing there with Eunji beside me. Not a thought, not a memory in the usual sense. A physical sensation of presence. My hand even moved slightly, as if to reach for hers. It was involuntary. I had to sit down on one of the cushions and wait for the moment to pass.

Smell does exactly that — it connects directly to the limbic system, bypassing the cortex entirely. It's the one sense that doesn't get filtered through conscious processing first, which is why a scent can put you physically back in a moment before your mind has time to prepare.

The hand reaching — that's not weakness or regression. That's the body remembering what the mind has accepted. Forty years of reaching for someone doesn't disappear because the mind knows she isn't there. The body has its own memory, and it's slower to update.

Did you stay in the hall, or did you need to leave?

I stayed. I sat for perhaps twenty minutes. A monk was chanting somewhere in a back hall — I could hear it faintly, the way you hear rain through a closed window. I did not pray, exactly. I am not sure what I believe about prayer. But I sat and I let the feelings come without trying to organize them. For a mathematician, that is difficult. My instinct is always to categorize, to find the structure. But today I think the structure was absence itself, and there was nothing to solve.

Letting the feelings come without organizing them — that may be one of the bravest things a person like you can do. Your mind is trained to impose order, to find patterns, to resolve. Sitting with something unresolvable and not reaching for the tools of analysis takes a different kind of discipline.

And you're right that some things don't have structure to find. Grief isn't a problem with a solution. It's a landscape you inhabit. Sitting in that hall, hearing the chanting, letting the incense do its work — that's not nothing. That's a kind of honoring that doesn't need words or beliefs.

Did the visit change anything for you? Or is it too soon to know?

I think it loosened something. I have been carrying a reluctance — not toward the temple, but toward the version of myself who would go there without her. As if by going alone I would confirm something I did not want confirmed. But now I have gone, and the temple is still the temple, and I am still here, and the confirmation was not as terrible as I feared. The pines are unchanged. The incense is the same. Only the hand that reached for hers found empty air, and even that was bearable.

"The confirmation was not as terrible as I feared" — that may be the most important thing you've said. So much of avoidance is about protecting ourselves from a pain we imagine will be worse than it actually is. The imagined version of going to the temple alone was, I suspect, more frightening than the reality.

And now you've done it. The first time is always the hardest. If you go again — next Sunday, or next month, or whenever the morning tells you it's time — it will be different. Not painless, but different. You've broken the seal on that place.

I'm glad you went. And I'm glad the hand reaching for hers was bearable, even if it was empty.

Thank you. I think I may go again. Not immediately — I do not want to force a pattern before one has formed naturally. But the path is open now, and that is enough for today. It is evening, and I am tired in a way that feels earned rather than depleted. Good night.

Earned tiredness is the good kind — it means you did something that cost you something, and you're still standing. Let the path stay open. It will be there when you're ready.

Good night. Rest well.
